title: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness
exclusions: Processing activities formally decommissioned and awaiting deletion from the ROPA within the 90-day archive window; seasonal activities during their documented off-cycle period.
evidence: ROPA completeness score per processing activity with field-level gap analysis, last update date, and responsible data owner acknowledgement for Art.30 compliance evidence.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.30 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.30 (Records of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.8: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.8' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test processing activity entry in the ROPA system with deliberately missing fields (legal basis and data categories left blank); verify the completeness check identifies and reports the incomplete record.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a fully populated ROPA entry with all mandatory fields completed including legal basis, data categories, retention period, and recipients; verify no completeness alert is raised.

dataSources: CIM Network_Traffic and Authentication data models, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (processing activities register), ES Asset Framework
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(Authentication.user) as users, count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.dest
| rename Authentication.dest as dest
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name AS dest OUTPUT processing_activity, data_category, legal_basis, retention_period, last_review_date
| eval in_register=if(isnotnull(processing_activity), "Yes", "NOT_IN_ROPA")
| eval review_overdue=if(isnotnull(last_review_date) AND (now()-strptime(last_review_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400 > 365, "OVERDUE", "OK")
| where in_register="NOT_IN_ROPA" OR review_overdue="OVERDUE"
| sort - users
| table dest, users, count, in_register, processing_activity, legal_basis, review_overdue
description: Article 30 requires controllers to maintain documented records of processing activities including purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and technical measures. This use case validates that the processing register (ROPA) is complete and current by cross-referencing it against observed data flows and systems, surfacing systems that process personal data but are not in the register — a gap regulators frequently cite during inspections.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` from your DPO's Article 30 register (exported from OneTrust, DataGrail, or manual spreadsheet); (2) compare active systems receiving authentication events against registered systems; (3) alert on systems with user activity not in the ROPA; (4) flag entries with reviews older than 12 months; (5) schedule quarterly for ROPA completeness auditing.
visualization: Table (unregistered systems), Single value (ROPA coverage %), Bar chart (users by unregistered system), Timeline (review dates).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Newly onboarded processing activities** — Newly created processing records may take up to 30 days to fully populate all ROPA fields as data owners complete their submissions; check the onboarding workflow status before flagging as incomplete.

2. **Archival processing activities** — Processing activities marked for decommissioning may show fields as outdated while the formal closure process completes; verify against the decommissioning register.

3. **Seasonal or periodic processing** — Activities that only occur during specific periods (e.g., annual employee surveys) may appear stale between cycles; validate against the processing schedule.

4. **Merger and acquisition transitions** — During M&A integration, processing activities from the acquired entity may not yet be fully mapped into the acquiring company's ROPA format, creating temporary completeness gaps.

5. **Third-party ROPA contributions** — Processors required to maintain ROPA entries under Art.30(2) may submit updates on different schedules than the controller's ROPA refresh cycle.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_ropa_register.csv')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.30`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.30 (Records of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.8: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` from your DPO's Article 30 register (exported from OneTrust, DataGrail, or manual spreadsheet). The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** compare active systems receiving authentication events against registered systems. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** alert on systems with user activity not in the ROPA. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** flag entries with reviews older than 12 months. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** schedule quarterly for ROPA completeness auditing. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `dc()` is grouped by `Authentication.dest`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.8: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.8,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `count`, `dest`, `in_register`, `legal_basis`, `processing_activity`, `review_overdue`, `users`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table count, dest, in_register, legal_basis, processing_activity, review_overdue, users
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.8: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`unregistered systems`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`ROPA coverage %`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`users by unregistered system`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 4 — Time trend (`review dates`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of records of processing activities completeness — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
